title: Instant withdrawals can burn full shares but return only a fraction of the owed ETH
claim: `StoneVault.instantWithdraw()` burns the caller's full STONE balance before it knows whether `StrategyController.forceWithdraw()` can actually source the requested ETH. The controller's `_forceWithdraw()` then asks each strategy for a fixed ratio slice of the requested amount instead of withdrawing against each strategy's real live balance, so drifted or illiquid strategies can return less than required while the user's entire share position is already destroyed.
impact: Users can suffer irreversible losses on instant withdrawals: their shares are fully burned, but they only receive the partial ETH amount the controller happened to recover.
paths: Strategy balances drift away from configured ratios because of yield, losses, or previous partial withdrawals.
A user calls `instantWithdraw(..., _shares)` and the vault computes the ETH owed for all burned shares.
The vault burns the full `_shares` amount before checking whether the controller can fund the withdrawal.
`StrategyController._forceWithdraw()` requests ratio-based amounts from each strategy, so underfunded or illiquid strategies return too little.
The vault pays only the partial ETH that came back, leaving the user with fewer assets and no remaining shares.

title: First depositor can capture any ETH already sitting in the system before round 1 starts
claim: Before the first rollover, `currentSharePrice()` hardcodes `1e18` whenever `latestRoundID == 0`, ignoring any ETH that is already present in `AssetsVault` or in strategies. A user depositing during this phase mints shares at par even if the system already holds seeded or accidentally transferred assets.
impact: Any preexisting ETH in the vault stack can be appropriated by the first public depositor once later rounds settle that value into share accounting.
paths: ETH is present in `AssetsVault` or a configured strategy before the first `rollToNextRound()`.
A user deposits while `latestRoundID == 0` and mints shares at the fixed 1 ETH/share bootstrap price.
Subsequent round rolls incorporate the previously ignored assets into `currentSharePrice()` and settlement accounting.
The bootstrap depositor later withdraws against a share base that now includes the seeded assets, extracting disproportionate value.

title: Remote bridge pause, cap, and price-feed messages always fail because STONE never handles its custom packet types
claim: `Stone` sends custom LayerZero payloads with packet types `PT_FEED`, `PT_SET_ENABLE`, and `PT_SET_CAP`, but it never overrides `OFTCore._nonblockingLzReceive()`. The inherited OFT implementation accepts only `PT_SEND` and reverts on every other packet type, so these admin and feed messages are stored as failed messages instead of being applied on the destination chain.
impact: Cross-chain safety controls are nonfunctional: remote bridge instances cannot actually be paused, have caps updated, or receive price-feed updates, leaving operators unable to enforce intended protections during incidents.
paths: A caller invokes `updatePrice()`, or the owner invokes `setEnableFor()` / `setCapFor()` for another chain.
`Stone` sends a LayerZero payload whose first field is `PT_FEED`, `PT_SET_ENABLE`, or `PT_SET_CAP`.
The destination contract routes the payload through inherited `OFTCore._nonblockingLzReceive()`.
`OFTCore` rejects any packet type other than `PT_SEND`, so the message fails and only lands in `failedMessages`.
The remote chain's `enable`, `cap`, or price state never changes.

title: A small instant withdraw can steal all ETH already stranded in StrategyController
claim: If `StrategyController` already holds ETH and that balance is at least the requested amount, `forceWithdraw()` repays the entire controller balance back to `AssetsVault` and returns that full balance as `actualAmount` instead of capping it to `_amount`. `StoneVault.instantWithdraw()` then credits the withdrawing user with that windfall.
impact: Any ETH that becomes stuck on the controller, such as delayed strategy payouts or forced transfers, can be stolen by the next user who performs even a small instant withdrawal.
paths: ETH accumulates on `StrategyController` outside the happy path.
A user triggers `instantWithdraw()` while vault idle liquidity is insufficient, so the vault calls `controller.forceWithdraw(requestedShortfall)`.
Because the controller's existing ETH balance already covers the request, `forceWithdraw()` repays and reports the full controller balance rather than the requested amount.
The vault adds that inflated `actualAmount` to the user's payout, transferring unrelated controller ETH to the withdrawer.

title: Post-settlement insolvency makes share-price math underflow and blocks recapitalization flows
claim: `currentSharePrice()` computes `assetsVault balance + strategy value - withdrawableAmountInPast` with no insolvency handling. If strategy losses reduce total assets below the already reserved past-withdrawal liability, that subtraction underflows and reverts.
impact: After a loss event, core flows that rely on `currentSharePrice()` can become uncallable, including fresh deposits and future round rolls, preventing recapitalization and trapping the vault in a broken state.
paths: A round is settled and `withdrawableAmountInPast` reserves assets for queued withdrawals.
Before those withdrawals are fully claimed, strategy value drops below the reserved liability.
A later `deposit()`, share-based `instantWithdraw()`, or `rollToNextRound()` calls `currentSharePrice()`.
The liabilities subtraction underflows, reverting the transaction and blocking the flow.

title: rollToNextRound is reentrant through strategy callbacks before round state is updated
claim: `StoneVault.rollToNextRound()` makes external calls into strategies through `StrategyController.rebaseStrategies()` before it updates `latestRoundID`, `settlementTime`, and withdrawal accounting, and the function is not protected by `nonReentrant`. A malicious or buggy strategy can call back into `rollToNextRound()` against stale state and cause nested round settlement.
impact: Nested round rolls can corrupt settlement sequencing and withdrawal accounting, potentially producing inconsistent prices or leaving users stuck behind broken round state.
paths: `rollToNextRound()` computes its preview values and then calls `controller.rebaseStrategies(...)`.
The controller calls a strategy's `deposit()`, `withdraw()`, or `clear()` implementation.
That strategy reenters `StoneVault.rollToNextRound()` before the outer call records the new round state.
Inner and outer executions settle rounds from different snapshots and then both write accounting fields.

title: Unvalidated strategy addresses can permanently brick pricing and rebalancing
claim: `StrategyController._initStrategies()` and `addStrategy()` accept arbitrary addresses without checking that they are real strategy contracts bound to this controller. The controller later blindly calls `getAllValue()`, `getInvestedValue()`, `withdraw()`, `instantWithdraw()`, `clear()`, and even `getAllValue()` again during destruction, so a bad address can make both normal operation and cleanup revert.
impact: A single mistaken or malformed strategy address can DoS price calculation, rebalancing, withdrawals, and even the path that is supposed to remove the bad strategy, causing a protocol-wide lockup until governance migrates away.
paths: An invalid or non-strategy address is supplied at deployment or later added through `addStrategy()`.
Core flows such as `currentSharePrice()` or `rollToNextRound()` iterate the strategy set and call into the bad address.
The external call reverts or returns malformed data, breaking the vault flow.
Attempts to destroy the bad strategy can also revert because `_couldDestroyStrategy()` calls `getAllValue()` on the same address.
